metadata.title: CWA Corrected Evaluation: Six Reviewer Fixes

examples.input: Compute corrected IFT/GELU peak memory ratio from stored benchmark values
examples.output: Corrected IFT/GELU ratio = 5.569x (was erroneously stored as 1.047x)
examples.metadata_bug_description: The stored field IFT_ratio_vs_GELU=1.046875 equals IFT_peak_MB (1.046875), not the ratio IFT_peak_MB/GELU_peak_MB. The code set ratio=IFT_peak_MB instead of ratio=IFT_peak_MB/GELU_peak_MB.
examples.metadata_anomaly_explanation: GELU baseline of 0.188 MB is unrealistically small for a standard activation benchmark because the single-layer batch=1 n=256 micro-benchmark is dominated by model parameter memory (~50K params × 4 bytes = 0.20 MB), not activation memory. This makes IFT's theoretical O(n) activation-memory advantage unmeasurable at this scale.

examples.input: Explain IFT/unrolled peak memory ratio = 1.0 in benchmark
examples.output: Ratio=1.0 is a measurement artifact: benchmark is model-parameter-dominated (0.188 MB params >> 0.0479 MB activation diff at n=256,K=50)
examples.metadata_explanation: When model parameter bytes (0.188 MB) >> activation bytes, both IFT and unrolled show identical peak memory because peak is set by parameter storage, not intermediate activations. At n=256, K=50, the activation memory difference is only 0.0479 MB — negligible vs 0.188 MB parameter memory. IFT's theoretical O(n) vs O(K·n) advantage requires large n.

examples.input: Reinterpret shift ablation results: CWA-Full vs CWA-ShiftOnly vs Pure-Tanh
examples.output: Full null result: Pure-Tanh (0.4731) > CWA-Full (0.4685) ≈ CWA-ShiftOnly (0.4686); all pairwise p > 0.05
examples.metadata_corrected_conclusion: 1) The self-consistent fixed-point coupling adds zero benefit over a detached mean-shift: CWA-Full≈CWA-ShiftOnly (p=0.9838, NOT significant). 2) CWA provides no statistically significant accuracy gain over Pure-Tanh (p=0.1263, NOT significant at α=0.05). 3) The shift itself does not improve accuracy over the no-shift baseline (Pure-Tanh numerically higher: 0.4731 > 0.4685), making the entire CWA mechanism a null result. 4) Because CWA-ShiftOnly exactly matches CWA-Full, the self-consistent coupling is NOT responsible for any observable behavior; even the partial confirmation that the shift explains the mechanism is itself refuted — the shift provides no accuracy benefit over Pure-Tanh.
examples.metadata_old_incorrect_claim: verdict='bias_dominant': coupling loss is entirely from mean shift; fixed-point adds no value. This framing implies the shift 'explains' CWA's behavior (partial credit). But the shift itself adds nothing over Pure-Tanh (p=0.171).
examples.metadata_new_verified_claim: Complete null result: neither the fixed-point coupling NOR the mean shift provides accuracy benefit. Pure-Tanh (no coupling, no shift) numerically outperforms both CWA variants (0.4731 vs 0.4686). All three pairwise tests non-significant at α=0.05.

examples.input: Compile explicit scope boundary for all empirical CWA claims
examples.output: All empirical claims in this study are scoped to: unnormalized MLPs (depths 6/10/20, hidden=256, no BatchNorm, no Dropout) on CIFAR-10 pixel vectors (ToTensor only, no augmentation), and a 6-layer cha
examples.metadata_scope_architectures: ['Unnormalized MLP (no BatchNorm, no Dropout) with depths 6, 10, 20', 'Hidden dimension = 256', '6-layer character-level GPT with hidden=256 (language model sub-experiment)', 'Fixed-J ablation: 10-layer unnormalized MLP with J ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9}']
examples.metadata_scope_datasets: ['CIFAR-10 (3072-dim pixel vectors, no augmentation, ToTensor only)', 'Tiny Shakespeare (character-level, ~1M chars)']
examples.metadata_out_of_scope_items: ['Architectures with BatchNorm', 'Architectures with residual connections (ResNet, skip connections)', 'Transformer architectures with LayerNorm (the GPT experiment is 6-layer only)', 'Hidden widths other than 256', 'Datasets other than CIFAR-10 and Tiny Shakespeare', 'Training regimes beyond 25 epochs (MLP) or 5000 steps (LM)', 'IFT memory efficiency claims at small n (n << 1000): benchmark is param-dominated', 'Warm-start bias claims outside K_warmup=3 initialization with ρ = J·s̄']
examples.metadata_scope_statement: All empirical claims in this study are scoped to: unnormalized MLPs (depths 6/10/20, hidden=256, no BatchNorm, no Dropout) on CIFAR-10 pixel vectors (ToTensor only, no augmentation), and a 6-layer character-level GPT (hidden=256) on Tiny Shakespeare. Results do NOT generalize to: normalized architectures (BatchNorm, LayerNorm), residual networks, hidden widths ≠ 256, datasets beyond CIFAR-10 and Tiny Shakespeare, or large-scale Transformer architectures. IFT memory efficiency claims require n >> model_params/bytes (n >> 50,000) to measure activation-dominated regimes. The warm-start bias bound (< 1%) holds at K_warmup=3 with ρ = J·s̄ ≈ 0.205 as empirically measured in the character-level LM sub-experiment.

examples.input: Compute correct warm-start bias using ρ = J·s̄ (Banach contraction rate) vs incorrect ρ = J
examples.metadata_derivation: The fixed-point iteration map m_{k+1} = T(m_k) has Lipschitz constant ‖DT‖ = J · mean(sech²(x + J·m*)) = J · s̄. By the Banach contraction theorem, |m_K - m*| ≤ ρ^K · |m_0 - m*| where ρ = J·s̄. Empirically: J·s̄ ≈ 0.2047 (mean of seeds [0.20449538777271906, 0.20480758820970854]), J ≈ 0.5182 (mean of seeds [0.5217009286085764, 0.5147042870521545]). Correct bias at K_warmup=3: ρ^3 = 0.2047^3 = 0.008571 = 0.8571%. Incorrect (using J alone): J^3 = 0.5182^3 = 0.139155 = 13.9155%. Overestimate factor: 16.24×. The correct bound confirms warm-start introduces negligible bias (< 1%) in practice.

examples.input: Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=cwa at depth=6
Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=relu at depth=6
Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=gelu at depth=6
Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=selu at depth=6
Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=competing_nl at depth=6
Compute abs_dev of gradient ratio for activation=gelu_ln at depth=6
examples.metadata_interpretation: At depth 6, GELU+LN shows abs_dev=0.6304 (rank 2 worst out of 6 activations). GELU+LN shows high abs_dev despite explicit per-layer normalization because log||∇W₁||/log||∇W_L|| conflates LayerNorm's internal magnitude re-scaling with true inter-layer gradient propagation, making the metric unreliable for normalized vs unnormalized architecture comparison at any depth.
